symptoms: Rectangular gray or tan lesions on leaves, running parallel to veins. Lesions have yellow halos. Severe cases cause entire leaves to dry out.
causes: Fungal infection caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis. Favored by high humidity, warm temperatures (25–30°C), and poor air circulation.

severity_indicators.mild: Less than 10% of leaf area affected
severity_indicators.severe: More than 50% of leaf area affected, stalks weakening

treatment.organic: Remove and destroy affected leaves. Apply neem oil spray (5ml per liter of water) every 7 days.
treatment.chemical: Apply mancozeb or azoxystrobin-based fungicide. Follow label instructions. Do not apply within 14 days of harvest.
treatment.cultural: Improve field drainage. Increase plant spacing for airflow. Rotate with non-host crops like soybean or sunflower.

prevention: Use certified disease-resistant seed varieties. Practice crop rotation. Avoid overhead irrigation. Apply fungicide preventively at tasseling stage.
yield_loss_estimate: Can reduce yield by 30–50% if untreated during grain fill stage.

symptoms: Irregular holes in leaves, ragged edges. Larvae visible on plants, especially at night. Damage starts at the top of the plant and moves downward.
causes: Insect pest caused by fall armyworm larvae. Migrates from warmer regions, thrives in maize fields with dense planting.

severity_indicators.moderate: 20–50% of leaves damaged, some whorl damage
severity_indicators.severe: More than 50% of plant damaged, including tassels and ears

treatment.organic: Hand-pick larvae and destroy. Introduce natural predators like parasitic wasps. Apply Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) spray.
treatment.chemical: Apply insecticides like lambda-cyhalothrin or chlorpyrifos. Follow label instructions and safety precautions.
treatment.cultural: Plant early to avoid peak pest migration. Use trap crops like Napier grass. Maintain field hygiene by removing crop residues.

prevention: Monitor fields regularly. Use resistant maize varieties. Practice intercropping with legumes. Avoid planting during peak migration periods.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause up to 70% yield loss in severe infestations if not controlled early.

symptoms: Yellow streaks along leaf veins, chlorotic mottling. Plants stunted, ears poorly developed. Leaves may curl or twist.
causes: Viral infection transmitted by leafhoppers (Cicadulina spp.). Spreads rapidly in fields with high vector populations.

severity_indicators.moderate: Streaks on multiple leaves, slight stunting
severity_indicators.severe: Heavy streaking, severe stunting, no ear formation

treatment.organic: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately. Use reflective mulches to deter leafhoppers.
treatment.chemical: Apply systemic insecticides to control leafhopper vectors. Use imidacloprid or thiamethoxam.
treatment.cultural: Plant resistant varieties. Time planting to avoid peak vector activity. Use clean seed and practice crop rotation.

prevention: Use certified virus-free seed. Control weeds that harbor vectors. Apply insecticides preventively. Plant border crops to trap vectors.
yield_loss_estimate: Can reduce yield by 20–100% depending on infection timing and severity.

symptoms: Brown discoloration at base of stalks, soft rot. Plants lodge easily. Ears may show pink mold.
causes: Fungal infection by Fusarium or Diplodia species. Favored by wet conditions and plant stress.

severity_indicators.moderate: Rot extending up the stalk, some lodging

treatment.organic: Improve soil drainage. Apply compost to strengthen plants. Remove infected residues.
treatment.chemical: Apply fungicides like tebuconazole at tasseling. Use seed treatments with fungicides.
treatment.cultural: Avoid over-fertilization with nitrogen. Ensure proper plant spacing. Harvest timely to reduce stress.

prevention: Use resistant varieties. Practice crop rotation. Avoid planting in low-lying areas. Apply balanced fertilizers.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause 10–30% yield loss due to lodging and reduced grain fill.

symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings on leaves. Yellow halos around spots. Fruit shows sunken lesions.
causes: Fungal infection by Alternaria solani. Spreads in humid, warm conditions with poor air circulation.

severity_indicators.moderate: Spots on multiple leaves, some defoliation

treatment.organic: Remove affected leaves. Apply copper-based fungicide spray every 7–10 days.
treatment.chemical: Use chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicides. Apply as per label instructions.
treatment.cultural: Mulch to prevent soil splash. Stake plants for better airflow. Water at base to avoid wetting leaves.

prevention: Use disease-resistant varieties. Rotate crops. Avoid overhead watering. Clean up plant debris after harvest.
yield_loss_estimate: Can reduce yield by 20–50% in severe cases.

symptoms: Water-soaked lesions on leaves and stems. White mold on undersides. Rapid plant collapse. Fruit shows brown rot.
causes: Fungal-like pathogen Phytophthora infestans. Thrives in cool, wet conditions.

treatment.organic: Remove infected plants immediately. Apply potassium bicarbonate spray.
treatment.chemical: Use metalaxyl or mefenoxam fungicides. Apply preventively in humid areas.
treatment.cultural: Improve drainage. Space plants for airflow. Avoid working in fields when wet.

prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Use fungicide-treated seeds. Monitor weather forecasts. Practice crop rotation.
yield_loss_estimate: Can destroy entire crops in epidemic conditions, 100% loss possible.

symptoms: Sudden wilting of plants, starting with lower leaves. Brown discoloration in vascular tissues when cut. No leaf spots.
causes: Bacterial infection spread through soil and water. Survives in soil for years.

treatment.organic: Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil. Use antagonistic bacteria like Pseudomonas.
treatment.chemical: No effective chemical control. Use soil fumigants like methyl bromide in severe cases.
treatment.cultural: Use clean transplants. Avoid planting in infected fields. Improve soil drainage.

prevention: Use resistant varieties. Practice long crop rotations (3–4 years). Disinfest tools and equipment.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause 20–80% yield loss depending on infection level.

symptoms: Dark, sunken lesions on blossom end of fruit. Fruit may crack. Not a true disease, but a physiological disorder.
causes: Calcium deficiency in fruit due to irregular watering or high soil salinity.

treatment.organic: Apply calcium-rich amendments like gypsum. Maintain consistent soil moisture.
treatment.chemical: Use calcium nitrate foliar sprays. Apply as needed.
treatment.cultural: Water regularly to avoid fluctuations. Mulch to maintain moisture. Avoid high nitrogen fertilizers.

prevention: Test soil calcium levels. Use balanced fertilizers. Maintain even watering schedule.

symptoms: Orange to reddish-brown pustules on leaves. Yellowing and defoliation. Pods may show lesions.
causes: Fungal infection by rust fungi. Spreads in humid conditions with moderate temperatures.

severity_indicators.moderate: Pustules on many leaves, some yellowing

treatment.organic: Remove infected leaves. Apply sulfur dust or neem oil.
treatment.chemical: Use triazole fungicides like tebuconazole. Apply preventively.
treatment.cultural: Improve airflow by proper spacing. Avoid overhead watering.

prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Practice crop rotation. Clean seed. Apply fungicides at first sign.
yield_loss_estimate: Can reduce yield by 20–60% in susceptible varieties.

symptoms: Yellowing and wilting of plants. Brown discoloration of roots. Stunted growth.
causes: Fungal pathogens like Fusarium and Rhizoctonia. Favored by wet soils and poor drainage.

treatment.organic: Improve drainage. Apply mycorrhizal fungi. Use compost.
treatment.chemical: Use fungicide-treated seeds. Apply soil fungicides like captan.
treatment.cultural: Avoid planting in wet areas. Rotate crops. Use raised beds.

prevention: Use resistant varieties. Practice long rotations. Ensure good soil drainage. Avoid overwatering.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause 10–50% yield loss depending on severity.

symptoms: Angular, water-soaked lesions on leaves. Lesions turn brown with yellow halos. Seeds may show spots.
causes: Bacterial infection spread by water splash and infected seeds.

severity_indicators.moderate: Lesions on many leaves, some defoliation

treatment.organic: Remove infected plants. Apply copper-based sprays.
treatment.chemical: Use copper hydroxide or streptomycin. Apply as per label.
treatment.cultural: Avoid overhead watering. Space plants for airflow. Use clean seed.

prevention: Use certified disease-free seed. Rotate crops. Avoid working in wet fields.
yield_loss_estimate: Can reduce yield by 15–40% in affected fields.

symptoms: Brown streaks in storage roots. Yellowing and wilting of leaves. Necrotic lesions on stems.
causes: Viral infection by Cassava brown streak viruses. Spread by whiteflies and infected cuttings.

severity_indicators.mild: Mild streaking in roots, slight leaf symptoms

treatment.organic: Use clean planting material. Rogue infected plants.
treatment.chemical: No effective chemical control. Use insecticides for vector control.
treatment.cultural: Use resistant varieties. Practice phytosanitation. Avoid planting near infected fields.

prevention: Use virus-tested planting material. Control whitefly vectors. Plant resistant varieties.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause 20–70% yield loss in susceptible varieties.

symptoms: Mosaic patterns on leaves, yellowing, leaf distortion. Stunted growth. Reduced root yield.
causes: Viral infection by Cassava mosaic begomoviruses. Spread by whiteflies and infected cuttings.

severity_indicators.severe: Severe distortion, bushy appearance, low yield

treatment.organic: Remove infected plants. Use clean cuttings.
treatment.chemical: No chemical cure. Use insecticides for whitefly control.
treatment.cultural: Plant resistant varieties. Practice crop rotation. Control weeds.

prevention: Use virus-free planting material. Plant tolerant varieties. Manage whitefly populations.
yield_loss_estimate: Can reduce yield by 20–90% depending on variety and infection severity.

symptoms: Red to brown pustules on stems and leaves. Black telia in severe cases. Premature ripening.
causes: Fungal infection by Puccinia graminis. Spread by wind-borne spores.

treatment.organic: Remove alternate hosts. Apply sulfur dust.
treatment.chemical: Use triazole fungicides like propiconazole. Apply at flag leaf stage.
treatment.cultural: Plant resistant varieties. Practice crop rotation.

prevention: Use rust-resistant wheat varieties. Monitor fields. Apply fungicides preventively in high-risk areas.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause 10–70% yield loss in epidemics.

symptoms: Yellow stripes on leaves parallel to veins. Pustules with yellow spores. Severe defoliation.
causes: Fungal infection by Puccinia striiformis. Thrives in cool, humid conditions.

treatment.organic: Apply sulfur or neem oil sprays.
treatment.chemical: Use fungicides like tebuconazole or azoxystrobin. Apply at first sign.
treatment.cultural: Plant resistant varieties. Avoid dense planting.

prevention: Use resistant varieties. Monitor weather. Apply fungicides preventively.
yield_loss_estimate: Can reduce yield by 20–60% in susceptible varieties.

symptoms: White, powdery coating on leaves and stems. Yellowing and curling of leaves. Reduced grain fill.
causes: Fungal infection by Blumeria graminis. Favored by high humidity and moderate temperatures.

treatment.organic: Apply sulfur dust or bicarbonate sprays.
treatment.chemical: Use fungicides like triadimefon or myclobutanil.

prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Practice crop rotation. Monitor humidity levels.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause 5–30% yield loss in severe cases.
